---
title: "Archetypes of the Doom Debate"
subtitle: "Mapping AI Risk Viewpoints — Portrait Framework"
date: "March 2026"
author: "[Author TBD]"
---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Document Meta — editorial rationale"}
This document is a LessWrong post mapping viewpoints in the AI doom debate. The meta notes throughout are **editorial rationale** — they record why each section is shaped the way it is and what design decisions were made. Retain them for future revision reference.

**Settled structure:**

- **§I — Why This Map Exists:** 3 paragraphs. Three beats: the Bengio/Yudkowsky observation → p(doom) as insufficient locator → what the map does and doesn't do. Framing the third paragraph around the axes rather than a general "multi-dimensional" claim was a deliberate tightening from a 4-paragraph draft.
- **§II — The Underlying Axes:** Five axes selected for being cross-cutting and non-obvious to an LW audience. Axis 2 (emergent vs. design choice) is the most load-bearing; Axis 5 includes the time-window dimension as a second sub-axis.
- **§III — The Archetypes:** Seven portraits. Uniform shape by design — forces comparison, resists giving more space to favored views. All seven now have defamiliarizing observations. Numbered 1–7 in render order.
- **§IV — Three Cuts:** Mechanism, epistemological stance, core crux. The transition table makes the map dynamic rather than a static gallery.
- **§VI — What This Map Is Good For:** Brief closing. Deliberately no new content — the final line ("ask which crux they are treating as settled") is the payoff.

**Outstanding:** §V (author's own position, `chapters/position.qmd`) exists but is not yet included. Four TBDs remain for the author to fill before adding the include. All p(doom) citation markers resolved except where flagged inline.
:::

---

## I. Why This Map Exists

::: aside
**META:** 3 paragraphs. Beat 1: Bengio/Yudkowsky as the entry — two people with the same threat model reaching incompatible conclusions. This is more precise than the old "debate is multi-dimensional" opener, which was generic. Beat 2: p(doom) as a number fails to locate the argument (the 22%/2% example). Beat 3: what the map does (locates cruxes) and doesn't do (pick winners) — the axes are named here before the portraits, which previews §II–§IV. Prior draft had a redundant fourth paragraph making the "multi-dimensional" claim in the abstract; that was folded into beat 3.
:::

The debate about AI risk is structurally illegible. Yoshua Bengio and Eliezer Yudkowsky share the exact same threat model. Both believe a sufficiently capable AI system with misaligned goals, acting in the world, would be catastrophic. Yet one thinks the right response is to avoid building agentic systems, while the other believes that will inevitably fail and demands a globally enforced moratorium. They agree on the danger. They disagree on the prescription. From the outside, their debate looks like a disagreement about whether AI is a threat at all. It isn't. It is a disagreement about whether architectural choices can close the safety gap — one specific technical crux, with enormous policy consequences on either side.

Surface-level metrics continually mask these structural cruxes. Someone at 22% p(doom) and someone at 2% p(doom) can have almost no real disagreement, or completely incompatible ones, depending on which definitions of risk they are carrying and what causal claim they are actually making. The question "what's your p(doom)?" almost never locates the actual argument.

The debate operates on several genuinely independent axes: whether dangerous agency emerges by default or is a design choice, whether current AI behavior is relevant evidence about catastrophic risk or a distraction from it, whether the primary failure is a single optimizer winning or many actors racing with no coordination. This post draws a map of those axes and the positions that follow from them. It doesn't pick winners. A map of a debate isn't useful because it acts as an arbiter. It is useful because it makes the structural disagreements legible enough to address. The seven archetypes and five axes below attempt to locate the cruxes. The framework comes first.

---

{{< include chapters/axes_archetypes.qmd >}}

---

{{< include chapters/archetypes/intro.qmd >}}

{{< include chapters/archetypes/hard-doomer.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** If the Hard Doomer is right, we need a physical moratorium. But what if the danger isn't a single rapid explosion of capability, but a slow, irresistible accumulation of systemic power? Enter the Structural Doomer.
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/structural-doomer.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** The Structural Doomer assumes bad outcomes are the default result of optimization. But what if the structural danger is real, yet alignment is merely an extraordinarily hard engineering problem rather than a theoretical impossibility?
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/conditional-doomer.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** The Conditional Doomer worries about successfully aligning the system. But what if technical alignment isn't the primary bottleneck at all? What if unsupervised human competition guarantees catastrophe regardless of the math?
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/multipolar.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** The Multipolar Realist sees human coordination as the only check against dangerous racing dynamics. If human coordination is the core dependency, one logical extreme is to preemptively and legally ban the creation of autonomous agents globally.
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/safe-by-design.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** The Alarmist wants to urgently ban agentic architectures. But what if that entire architectural paradigm is a dead end that will never mathematically reach human-level agency anyway?
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/paradigm-skeptic.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** The Skeptic believes current models won't reach AGI. But what if they actually do, and instead of a sharp capability cliff or an explosion, the transition is just a continuous, mundane process of economic integration?
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/gradualist.qmd >}}

---

{{< include chapters/cuts_archetypes.qmd >}}

---

{{< include chapters/utility.qmd >}}
